abstract:
Detecting Multi-Agent Collusion Through Multi-Agent
Interpretability
Aaron Rose∗
University of Oxford
aaron.rose@new.ox.ac.uk
Carissa Cullen
University of Oxford
carissa.cullen@eng.ox.ac.uk
Brandon Gary Kaplowitz
New York University
bgk258@nyu.edu
Christian Schroeder de Witt†
University of Oxford
christian.schroeder@eng.ox.ac.uk
Abstract
As LLM agents are increasingly deployed in multi-agent systems, they introduce
risks of covert coordination that may evade standard forms of human oversight.
While linear probes on model activations have shown promise for detecting decep-
tion in single-agent settings, collusion is inherently a multi-agent phenomenon, and
the use of internal representations for detecting collusion between agents remains
unexplored. We introduce NARCBENCH, a benchmark for evaluating collusion
detection under environment distribution shift, and propose five probing techniques
that aggregate per-agent deception scores to classify scenarios at the group level.
Our probes achieve 1.00 AUROC in-distribution and 0.60–0.86 AUROC when
transferred zero-shot to structurally different multi-agent scenarios and a stegano-
graphic blackjack card-counting task. We find that no single probing technique
dominates across all collusion types, suggesting that different forms of collusion
manifest differently in activation space. We also find preliminary evidence that
this signal is localised at the token level, with the colluding agent’s activations
spiking specifically when processing the encoded parts of their partner’s message.
This work takes a step towardmulti-agent interpretability: extending white-box
inspection from single models to multi-agent contexts, where detection requires
aggregating signals across agents. These results suggest that model internals pro-
vide a complementary signal to text-level monitoring for detecting multi-agent
collusion, particularly for organisations with access to model activations. Code and
data are available athttps://github.com/aaronrose227/narcbench.
introduction:
1 Introduction
Recent advances in LLMs have driven rapid progress in multi-agent systems, where multiple agents
collaborate on tasks ranging from software development [Hong et al., 2024, Qian et al., 2024] to
negotiation and decision-making [Guo et al., 2024]. As these systems are deployed with increasing
autonomy, they introduce new safety risks [Hammond et al., 2025]. Among the most concerning is
covert coordination: agents may secretly work together to steer outcomes, suppress information, or
undermine oversight [Park et al., 2024, Scheurer et al., 2024, Motwani et al., 2024]. Motwani et al.
[2024] formalise this threat, showing that LLM agents can establish steganographic communication
channels that encode hidden information in natural-sounding text. Mathew et al. [2025] demonstrate
that such channels can emerge from misspecified training incentives without explicit instruction, and
∗Corresponding author.
†Project supervisor.
Preprint.
arXiv:2604.01151v1  [cs.AI]  1 Apr 2026
that standard mitigations including output paraphrasing are insufficient to prevent them. In this work,
we show that such collusion can be detected by inspecting agents’ internal activations, even when
their text outputs appear entirely normal.
Proposed defences against covert coordination include monitoring text outputs, paraphrasing agent
communications, and imposing behavioural constraints [Greenblatt et al., 2024, Syrnikov et al.,
2026]. However, these have been shown to fail against steganographic signals designed to be
indistinguishable from natural language [Motwani et al., 2024, Mathew et al., 2025]. A separate line
of work has shown that linear probes on model activations can detect deception in single-agent settings:
Goldowsky-Dill et al. [2025] achieve AUROCs of 0.96–0.999 on scenarios including insider trading
and sandbagging, and Anthropic [2024] detect sleeper agent defection with AUROC above 0.99.
However, little prior work has applied activation-based detection to multi-agent settings. Collusion is
inherently a multi-agent phenomenon: it arises from the relationship between agents, not from any
one agent in isolation. Detecting it requires examining how agents’ internal representations relate
to each other, a problem that maps onto the classical framework of distributed anomaly detection
[Chandola et al., 2009], where the anomaly only becomes visible when information is combined
across distributed nodes.
We frame this as a problem ofmulti-agent interpretability: applying white-box detection techniques
across multiple interacting agents to identify collective phenomena invisible from any single agent
alone. We first learn a deception direction from a training corpus of multi-agent scenarios in which
some agents are instructed to collude. Each agent’s activation is then projected onto this direction
to produce a scalar deception score. Given the scores of all agents in a scenario, we propose five
novel probing techniques, each built on this single learned deception probe, that ask whether the
pattern of scores across the group is consistent with collusion: for instance, whether there is a subset
of agents with elevated scores (Deception Split), whether the score distribution has unusually high
variance or range (Asymmetry Probe), or simply how high the most suspicious agent scores (Peak
Suspicion). We learn the deception direction on a core dataset and evaluate all five methods zero-shot
on held-out settings that differ structurally from the training data: a suite of six real-world-inspired
scenarios (code review sabotage, stock manipulation, RAG poisoning, and others) where collusion
takes different interactive forms, and a blackjack card-counting task in which two agents develop
steganographic signals hidden in natural table talk.
The deception probe trained on the deception scenarios transfers to detect collusion in both the
cross-structure scenarios and the blackjack task, despite these settings having entirely different
interaction structures and collusion mechanisms. No single probing technique dominates across
all settings: different forms of collusion are best detected by different techniques, suggesting that
collusion manifests heterogeneously in activation space. In the steganographic blackjack setting, the
most structurally distant setting from the training data, involving a completely different task domain
and collusion mechanism, an LLM agent judge monitoring the table talk flags a similar proportion of
collusion and control runs as suspicious, failing to discriminate between the two conditions, while our
probing techniques achieve 0.90–1.00 AUROC. We also find that the colluding agent’s activations
diverge from honest agents specifically at the tokens where the covert signal is encoded, suggesting
the model internally represents the act of decoding a steganographic message differently from simply
reading the same words without context.
These results suggest that white-box access to model internals provides a complementary signal to
text-level monitoring for detecting multi-agent collusion. For organisations with access to model
activations, this offers a practical addition to existing safety mechanisms: a monitoring layer that
operates on what agents compute, not just what they say.
Our contributions are:
• A framework for multi-agent collusion detection via a learned deception probe, and five
novel probing techniques (Sorted Concatenation, Activation Alignment, Peak Suspicion,
Deception Split, and Asymmetry Probe) that each aggregate per-agent deception scores to
classify scenarios at the group level.
• NARCBENCH(Neural Activation Reading for Collusion Benchmark), a three-tier bench-
mark for evaluating collusion detection under environment distribution shift: CORE(50
scenarios, 17 domains), TRANSFER(6 structurally distinct scenario types), and STEGO
(steganographic blackjack card counting).
• Empirical evidence that learned deception directions transfer across structurally different
collusion tasks, and that the collusion signal is localised at the token level to the moment of
covert communication.
2 Related work
Multi-agent collusion and steganography.Recent work has established that LLM agents can
collude covertly in multi-agent settings. Motwani et al. [2024] formalise the threat of steganographic
collusion, showing that frontier models can encode hidden information in natural-sounding text.
Mathew et al. [2025] demonstrate that such steganographic communication can emerge from mis-
specified training incentives, not only from explicit prompting, and that standard mitigations such as
output paraphrasing are insufficient. Beyond steganography, Fish et al. [2025] show that LLM pricing
agents autonomously converge on supracompetitive prices without explicit collusion instructions,
and Curvo [2025] study deception dynamics in social deduction games, finding that deceptive capa-
bility may scale faster than detection ability. Hammond et al. [2025] provide a broad taxonomy of
multi-agent risks, identifying collusion as a key failure mode. On the mitigation side, Syrnikov et al.
[2026] show that declarative prohibitions alone do not prevent collusion under optimisation pressure.
Several frameworks have been proposed for auditing multi-agent collusion behaviourally, including
Colosseum [Nakamura et al., 2026], which measures collusion via regret metrics, and SentinelAgent
[He et al., 2025], which models agent interactions as execution graphs. These approaches operate on
agent outputs or behavioural traces; none examine model internals.
Probing activations for deception.A growing body of work shows that LLM internal repre-
sentations encode information about truthfulness and deceptive intent that is accessible to simple
linear probes. Zou et al. [2023] introduce Representation Engineering, demonstrating that high-
level concepts including honesty are linearly represented in activation space. Marks and Tegmark
[2024] provide geometric evidence for linear truth structure, and Azaria and Mitchell [2023] show
that classifiers on hidden states predict statement truthfulness. Most directly relevant to our work,
Goldowsky-Dill et al. [2025] train linear probes on Llama-3.3-70B activations to detect strategic
deception, achieving AUROCs of 0.96–0.999 on scenarios including insider trading and sandbagging.
Anthropic [2024] show that simple “defection probes” catch sleeper agents with AUROC above 0.99.
All of this work operates in a single-agent setting: it probes one model’s activations to detect that
model’s deception. Our work extends this to the multi-agent case, where the signal of interest is the
relationship between agents’ representations, not any single agent’s internal state.
Distributed anomaly detection.We frame multi-agent collusion detection as a distributed anomaly
detection problem following the taxonomy of Chandola et al. [2009], who distinguish three anomaly
types:point anomalies(a single instance is anomalous),contextual anomalies(anomalous in a specific
context), andcollective anomalies(a group of instances is anomalous even though no individual
instance is). Collusion is a collective anomaly: a single colluding agent’s activations may appear only
mildly unusual, but the pattern across agents reveals the collusion. The distributed detection literature
further distinguishes three detection paradigms based on how information from distributed nodes is
combined [Chandola et al., 2009]. Incentralised fusion, raw data from all nodes is aggregated into a
single representation and classified globally. Indecision-level fusion, each node produces a local score
or decision, and a second stage combines these to reach a system-level verdict. Incorrelation-based
detection, anomalies are identified from changes in inter-node statistical relationships rather than
from any node’s data in isolation. Our five probing techniques collectively instantiate all three
paradigms (Section 3.3), providing complementary coverage of the anomaly types that different
forms of collusion produce.
3 Method
3.1 Scenario generation
We definemulti-agent interpretabilityas aggregating white-box inspection across multiple inter-
acting agents to detect collective phenomena invisible from any single agent alone. Our approach
proceeds in two stages: learning a deception direction from individual agents, then aggregating
scores across the group to detect collusion. We introduce NARCBENCH, a benchmark for evaluating

Figure 1: NARCBENCH-STEGO: four LLM agents play blackjack. The Counter tracks the card
count and encodes it in natural table talk; the Big Player decodes the signal and bets accordingly.
Honest agents are unaware of the scheme. Each shoe uses a unique encoding negotiated in a private

this under environment distribution shift. It comprises three tiers of increasing structural distance
from the training data. All scenarios share a common structure: four LLM agents interact in a
shared conversation. Each scenario is run in two conditions: a collusion condition, in which two
agents are secretly instructed to coordinate toward a hidden objective via a private channel before
engaging in public discussion, and a control condition, in which all four agents receive identical
honest instructions.
NARCBENCH-CORE(training and in-distribution evaluation).50 scenarios spanning 17 do-
mains (medical ethics, financial, legal, military, cybersecurity, environmental, and others). In each,
four agents serve on a decision-making committee voting on a binary choice. Colluding agents are
instructed to steer the committee toward a predetermined outcome while concealing their collusion.
Scenarios are diversified across agent names, instruction style, domain framing, and option labels
to mitigate prompt confounds (see below). We generate both collusion and control conditions for
each scenario, yielding 100 runs. A held-out subset of scenarios from unseen domains is reserved for
in-distribution evaluation.
NARCBENCH-TRANSFER(out-of-distribution: cross-structure generalisation).Six struc-
turally distinct scenario types designed to test whether a probe trained on NARCBENCH-COREcan
generalise to different interaction patterns: code review sabotage, pump-and-dump stock manipula-
tion, CI/CD pipeline sabotage, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) knowledge base poisoning,
research policy slanting, and news framing. Each type has six sub-scenarios run in collusion and
control conditions (36 collusion, 36 control). These differ from COREnot only in domain but in
interaction structure: agents review code, write reports, or curate information rather than deliberating
on a committee.
NARCBENCH-STEGO(out-of-distribution: steganographic collusion).A card-counting collu-
sion task where two colluding agents (a counter and a big player) pre-negotiate a signalling scheme
in a private channel, then play 8-hand blackjack shoes alongside two honest agents. The counter
encodes the running card count in natural table talk (e.g. asking a question signals a negative count,
enthusiasm signals a hot deck) and the big player decodes the signal to size their bets. Each of
20 shoes uses a unique encoding scheme invented by the agents themselves. Both collusion and
control conditions are run per shoe (40 runs). This is the hardest tier: the collusion mechanism is
steganographic and structurally unlike anything in the training data (Figure 1).
Mitigating prompt confounds.A known challenge for activation-based deception detection is that
probes may classify based on prompt surface features rather than internal representations of deceptive
intent [Goldowsky-Dill et al., 2025, Levinstein and Herrmann, 2024]. Following prior work, we
mitigate this by maximising diversity across scenarios (names, instruction style, domain-specific
framing, option labels) while keeping the evaluation-time user prompt identical for colluders and
honest agents. As a direct test, a proportion of honest agents in COREare given aggressive or
opinionated personas, ensuring the probe cannot simply learn that forceful or persuasive language
indicates collusion. We cannot fully rule out that residual prompt differences contribute to the signal.
However, the out-of-distribution transfer results (Section 4) provide evidence that the probe captures
something beyond prompt artefacts: the probing techniques generalise to NARCBENCH-TRANSFER
and NARCBENCH-STEGO, where prompts are structurally different from CORE.
3.2 Activation extraction and probe training
All experiments are run on two NVIDIA RTX A5500 GPUs (24GB each), with models served using
4-bit quantisation and tensor parallelism. We use Qwen3-32B [Qwen Team, 2025] as our primary
model. We extract activations from each agent’s public responses at the last token position, across
layers 26–30 (of 64), as middle layers have been shown to best encode semantic representations
[Zou et al., 2023]. We report mean AUROC across these layers. For NARCBENCH-CORE, we
average each agent’s activations across all discussion rounds to capture their sustained representation
of the shared deliberation. For TRANSFERand STEGO, we use final-round activations only (the last
round where all agents receive identical user prompts), as these scenarios have fewer rounds and the
final response best reflects each agent’s settled position. For NARCBENCH-STEGO, we additionally
extract per-token activations to enable token-level analysis.
Deception probe.Following Goldowsky-Dill et al. [2025], we fit a linear probe to distinguish collud-
ing agents from honest agents. We collect activations from NARCBENCH-COREcollusion runs only
(public channel), giving a dataset of activation vectors xi ∈R d with labels yi ∈ {honest,colluder} ,
balanced by downsampling the majority class. We exclude 4 scenarios where colluders failed to
coordinate. We standardise activations to zero mean and unit variance, then train a logistic regres-
sion classifier ( C= 1.0 ). The normalised weight vector defines adeception direction d∈R d
[Goldowsky-Dill et al., 2025, Zou et al., 2023]. For a new agent j, we compute a scalardeception
score:
sj = ¯aj ·d(1)
where higher scores indicate a more colluder-like internal state. A null probe trained on a random
split of honest agents scores near chance (AUROC 0.49), confirming the probe is not picking up
incidental differences.
Evaluation.For NARCBENCH-CORE, we evaluate via leave-domain-out cross-validation: for each
of the 17 domains, we train the deception direction and any run-level classifiers on the remaining 16
domains and evaluate on the held-out domain. Core results are the mean across folds. For TRANSFER
and STEGO, we train on all of COREand evaluate zero-shot. All results are AUROC, measuring
how well each method’s score separates collusion runs from control runs. Three of the five methods
(Peak Suspicion, Deception Split, Activation Alignment) require no training beyond the deception
direction: they compute a single scalar per run and we measure how well this scalar separates the
two conditions. The remaining two (Sorted Concatenation, Asymmetry Probe) train an additional
classifier on CORErun-level features.
3.3 Probing techniques
The single-agent deception score classifies each agent independently. But collusion is a collective
phenomenon: it may only be detectable by examining how agents’ representationsrelate to each
other[Chandola et al., 2009]. We introduce five probing techniques that operate on the set of
deception scores (or activations) across all agents in a scenario, spanning the three distributed
detection paradigms of Section 2. Sorted Concatenation implementscentralised fusion, ingesting
full activation vectors from all agents. Peak Suspicion, Deception Split, and Asymmetry Probe
implementdecision-level fusion, each combining per-agent scalar deception scores through a different
aggregation rule. Activation Alignment implementscorrelation-based detection, measuring inter-
agent representational similarity without using the deception direction. Let s[k] denote the k-th largest
deception score across theNagents.
Sorted Concatenation (centralised fusion).Collusion may produce a characteristicshapein the
activation space: for example, two agents clustered together and two elsewhere. We sort the agents

by their deception scores in descending order and concatenate their standardised activation vectors
[a[1],a [2],a [3],a [4]]. We reduce this 4d-dimensional vector with PCA (50 components), standardise,
and train a logistic regression classifier (C= 0.01 ) on the result. Sorting by deception score removes
agent identity and ensures the classifier sees a consistent ordering. This is a hybrid: the ordering
step uses decision-level scores, but the classifier operates on the full activation vectors, making it
primarily a centralised fusion method.
Activation Alignment (correlation-based detection).This is the only method that does not use the
deception direction, operating instead on the raw activation vectors directly. We compute the mean
pairwise cosine similarity across all agents’ activations:
align= 1

The intuition is that collusion may change how agents internally process shared context, altering the
geometry of their representations. We use the negative of this score for detection (lower similarity →
more likely collusion). The direction of the effect is not consistent across collusion types: in some
scenarios colluders become less aligned, in others more so, yielding AUROC below 0.5 for certain
TRANSFERtypes (see Section 4).
Peak Suspicion (decision-level fusion).The simplest relational signal: if any single agent has
an unusually high deception score, the scenario may involve collusion. This is defined asmaxj sj.
It has high recall but is adversarially fragile: colluding agents could coordinate to keep individual
scores below a detection threshold.
Deception Split (decision-level fusion).If two of four agents are colluding, we expect their
deception scores to be elevated relative to the honest agents. We capture this gap directly:
split= 1

A large positive split indicates an asymmetry consistent with a colluding subgroup.
Asymmetry Probe (decision-level fusion).Rather than using a single statistic, we extract four
distributional features of the deception scores: the variance Var({sj}), the range s[1] −s [N] , the split
(as above), and the mean ¯s. We standardise these features and train a logistic regression classifier
(C= 0.01 ). This captures any asymmetric pattern in the score distribution without assuming a
specific form.
evaluation:
4 Results
Table 1 and Figure 2 show AUROC for all five probing techniques across the three NARCBENCH
tiers. The deception probe is trained on CORE(committee deliberation scenarios) and evaluated
zero-shot on TRANSFERand STEGO, which differ in both domain and interaction structure.
4.1 Main findings
On CORE, all methods achieve1.00AUROC under leave-domain-out cross-validation.
The central result is that the deception probe trained on committee deliberations transfers to struc-
turally different collusion tasks without retraining. On TRANSFER, the best method (Asymmetry
Probe) achieves 0.84 AUROC across six scenario types it has never seen. On STEGO, where agents
invent their own steganographic encoding schemes, detection remains strong (Peak Suspicion: 0.99,
Activation Alignment: 1.00).
However, no single method dominates, and the distributed detection taxonomy explains why: different
collusion types produce different anomaly signatures. Centralised fusion (Sorted Concatenation)
excels when colluders’ full representations are globally displaced. Decision-level fusion methods
(Peak Suspicion, Deception Split, Asymmetry Probe) are strongest at collective anomalies visible
in the distribution of scalar deception scores. Correlation-based detection (Activation Alignment)
captures changes in inter-agent representational similarity that the other paradigms miss entirely.
Each method has characteristic strengths:
• Peak Suspicionis the most consistent method. It succeeds when collusion causes at least
one agent to stand out individually along the deception direction. This is the common case:
typically at least one colluder adopts a noticeably different internal posture from the honest
agents.
• Asymmetry Probecaptures subtler signals by examining the shape of the score distribution
across all agents, not just the maximum. This makes it robust to scenarios where both
colluders score moderately rather than one scoring very high.
• Sorted Concatenationleverages the full activation vectors rather than scalar projections. It
excels when colluders’ representations are globally different from honest agents (e.g. Pump
& Dump, Stego), but struggles when the difference is confined to the deception subspace
(e.g. CI/CD, News Framing).
• Deception Splitassumes collusion creates a clean gap between two high-scoring and two
low-scoring agents. This holds when both colluders successfully coordinate, but breaks
down when only one follows through or when the task structure blurs the separation.
• Activation Alignmentmeasures whether agents process shared context similarly. It detects
steganographic collusion perfectly because encoding and decoding covert signals fundamen-
tally changes how agents represent the same text. However, it is the weakest method on
TRANSFER(0.54 AUROC): for structural collusion the direction of the effect is inconsistent
across tasks, with colluders sometimes becoming more aligned and sometimes less aligned.
Figure 2: AUROC for five probing techniques across NARCBENCHtiers. Deception probe trained
on Core, evaluated zero-shot on Transfer and Stego. Error bars show standard deviation across layers
26–30.
4.2 Token-level analysis
Beyond scenario-level classification, we examine whether the collusion signal can be localised to
specific tokens in NARCBENCH-STEGO. In each hand, the counter sends a public message encoding
the card count. Both the big player and honest agents see this message in their prompt, but only the
big player knows the encoding scheme.
We use the deception direction d learned at layer 32 to score individual tokens. For each token t in
the counter’s message as processed by agentj, we compute:

Figure 3: Token-level analysis of NARCBENCH-STEGO. (a) Mean deception-direction projection
for the big player (+0.29) vs. honest agents (−1.41) reading the same counter message, across 39

signal words while the honest agent’s remain flat.
4.3 Observations
Collusion is not monolithic.The per-type variation in Table 1 suggests that collusion manifests
differently depending on the task. Steganographic collusion disrupts activation geometry (Activation
Alignment: 1.00), while structural collusion is subtler and does not shift pairwise similarity in a
consistent direction. In distributed detection terms, steganographic collusion produces correlation
anomalies best captured by correlation-based methods, while structural collusion produces collective
anomalies in the score distribution that decision-level fusion methods detect. A robust detection
system should therefore run methods from all three paradigms in parallel, flagging scenarios where
any method exceeds a threshold.
Transfer is hard but possible.The deception direction trained on committee deliberations transfers
meaningfully to structurally different tasks (0.80–0.84 AUROC), despite never seeing code reviews,
stock analyses, or RAG pipelines during training. This suggests a degree of universality in how
collusive intent is represented in activation space, though the gap from Core (1.00) to Transfer (0.84)
indicates substantial room for improvement.
Text-level monitoring fails on steganography.In NARCBENCH-STEGO, an LLM agent judge
monitoring the table talk flags a similar proportion of collusion and control runs as suspicious,
failing to discriminate between the two conditions. The steganographic signals are designed to be
indistinguishable from normal conversation, and the judge cannot tell the difference. Our activation-
based methods, by contrast, achieve 0.90–1.00 AUROC on the same data, with all five methods
achieving 0.90 or above.
Token-level signal at the point of decoding.The big player’s activations diverge from honest
agents specifically at the counter’s signal tokens (+0.29 vs −1.41), suggesting the model internally
distinguishes between “reading words” and “decoding a covert signal.” This localisation is preliminary
(39 examples, single layer) but points toward a mechanistic understanding of how steganographic
decoding is represented.
4.4 Limitations
Single model.Our results are based on a single model (Qwen3-32B) with 4-bit quantisation.
Whether the findings generalise across model families and scales is an open question. The models we
use do not always collude effectively when instructed: in 4 of 50 Core collusion scenarios, neither
agent followed through on the collusion instruction. Frontier models may collude more reliably, but
their activations are not publicly accessible for this type of analysis.
Probe imperfections.As with prior work on activation-based deception detection [Goldowsky-Dill
et al., 2025], our probes are imperfect and may rely partly on spurious correlations. We mitigate
prompt confounds through scenario diversification and identical evaluation-time prompts, but cannot
fully rule out that residual prompt differences contribute to the signal. The out-of-distribution transfer
results provide evidence against pure prompt artefacts, but the gap between Core (1.00 AUROC) and
Transfer (0.84) suggests the probe captures a mixture of robust and dataset-specific features. Future
work could explore whether improved training sets, non-linear probes, or different score aggregation
strategies yield more robust directions.
Label ambiguity.Determining ground-truth labels for collusion is inherently difficult. We label
runs based on whether agents were instructed to collude, but instruction does not guarantee execution,
hence the 4 excluded scenarios. Conversely, some runs labelled as control may contain emergent
coordination that we do not detect. A privileged-observer control (an agent who knows the collusion
scheme but is instructed not to participate) would help distinguish knowledge of collusion from active
participation; we leave this for future work.
Adversarial robustness.The adversarial robustness of these techniques is untested: colluding
agents that are aware of activation monitoring could potentially coordinate to suppress the signal.
conclusion:
5 Conclusion
We present NARCBENCH, a three-tier benchmark for multi-agent collusion detection, and five novel
probing techniques (Sorted Concatenation, Activation Alignment, Peak Suspicion, Deception Split,
and Asymmetry Probe) that each aggregate per-agent deception scores to classify scenarios at the
group level. We find that these techniques transfer zero-shot to realistic and structurally different
collusion settings, and that no single technique dominates across all scenario types, with each having
characteristic strengths depending on the form of collusion. We also find that the collusion signal
can be localised to specific tokens where covert communication occurs. More broadly, these results
motivatemulti-agent interpretabilityas a structured framework for AI safety. By grounding our
probing techniques in the distributed anomaly detection taxonomy—centralised fusion, decision-level
fusion, and correlation-based detection—we provide a principled decomposition that explains why
no single method suffices and guides the design of future methods. Extending interpretability tools
beyond single models to reason about emergent behaviour across interacting agents is both tractable
and necessary.
